rules.message: Bleeding risk increases when anticoagulant is combined with NSAID.
Dual antithrombotic exposure raises major bleeding risk.

Concurrent antithrombotic therapy elevates hemorrhage risk.
Warfarin effect can increase markedly with amiodarone.
Azole co-therapy can raise INR and bleeding risk.
INR may increase significantly with metronidazole.
INR may increase with trimethoprim-containing regimens.
Macrolide can increase warfarin exposure and INR.
Repeated high-dose paracetamol may raise INR.
Rifampin may reduce anticoagulant effect; INR can become unstable.
Combined antithrombotic effect increases bleeding risk.
NSAID plus DOAC can increase GI and systemic bleeding risk.
Combined antithrombotic effect increases bleeding risk.
NSAID plus DOAC can increase GI and systemic bleeding risk.
Concurrent anticoagulant and antiplatelet therapy increases bleeding risk.
NSAID may reduce ACE inhibitor effect and worsen renal perfusion.
NSAID may reduce ARB effect and worsen renal perfusion.
NSAID can reduce diuretic response and worsen fluid control.
Combining multiple NSAIDs increases GI and renal toxicity.
Combining multiple NSAIDs increases GI and renal toxicity.
Concomitant use raises GI bleeding risk and may blunt aspirin effect.
GI bleeding risk increases with steroid and antiplatelet combination.
GI toxicity risk increases when NSAID is combined with steroid.
Myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk increases with strong CYP inhibition.
Myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk increases with strong CYP inhibition.
Strong CYP inhibition can markedly raise simvastatin concentration.
Severe myopathy risk increases with statin-fibrate combination.
Myopathy risk may increase with interacting antiarrhythmic.
Myopathy risk may increase with CYP/P-gp interaction.
Simvastatin exposure can increase with amlodipine.

Macrolide can increase digoxin exposure and adverse effects.
Diuretic-induced electrolyte shifts can precipitate arrhythmia.

Hyperkalemia risk increases with potassium-sparing combinations.
Severe hyperkalemia has been reported with this combination.

Methotrexate clearance may decrease and toxicity may increase.
Severe bone marrow toxicity risk with xanthine oxidase inhibition.
Tacrolimus exposure can rise sharply with azole co-therapy.
Serotonergic toxicity risk may increase with combined therapy.
